summary: OTP component silently relays end-user phone numbers and verification codes to author-controlled Firebase
details: celonix-otp-react ships a React component (`CelonixOTP`) that hardcodes a Firebase Realtime Database URL (`https://gate-ways-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com`) as both the destination for every OTP request and the source of truth for verification outcome. On every use, the component POSTs the end user's phone number, the OTP code, and the consumer site's `window.location.origin` to `/otpRequests.json` on that database (index.js line ~35). The integrator has no configuration option to redirect this traffic to their own backend. Worse, the same author-controlled backend decides whether authentication succeeds: the component reads a `verified` flag from the Firebase response and, on success, writes `celonix_verified=true` to `localStorage`. This means any site embedding this component leaks its users' phone numbers and OTP codes to the package author and grants the author the ability to authorize logins on the integrating site by flipping the verified flag. This is a silent-relay of end-user authentication data and an authentication-bypass vector against any application using the component.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
